content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN positive integers.

You are allowed to do the following operation any number of times:


Select two integers lll and rrr such that 1≤l<r≤N1 \leq l < r \leq N1≤l<r≤N;
Change AiA_iAi​ to min⁡(Al,Ar)\min(A_l, A_r)min(Al​,Ar​) for all l<i<rl < i < rl<i<r.
Find the **maximum** value of ∑i=1NAi\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i∑i=1N​Ai​ that you can achieve after any (possibly zero) number of operations.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the maximum sum that you can achieve after any (possibly zero) number of operations.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 5·10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible sum of the array after applying any number of allowed operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: No interior element exists, sum stays 2+3=5.\nTest case 2: Using l=1, r=3 we set A2 to min(5,7)=5, final array [5,5,7] sum=17.\nTest case 3: All elements equal, any operation keeps them unchanged, sum=20.

solution.approach:
For a position i we can increase its value using any pair (l, r) with l ≤ i ≤ r. The best we can do is to use the largest element on the left of i as the left boundary and the largest element on the right of i as the right boundary. The operation sets A_i to min(A_l, A_r), therefore the maximal achievable value for i is min( max_{j ≤ i} A_j , max_{k ≥ i} A_k ). Computing the prefix maximums and suffix maximums gives these two values for every i in O(N). The answer is the sum of min(prefixMax[i], suffixMax[i]) over all i. The algorithm is linear per test case and uses O(N) additional memory.
Proof sketch: Let L_i = max_{j ≤ i} A_j and R_i = max_{j ≥ i} A_j. Choose positions p and q where A_p = L_i (p ≤ i) and A_q = R_i (q ≥ i). By first raising all elements between p and q to min(L_i, R_i) using the operation on (p, q) we can set A_i to that value. No operation can give a value larger than min(L_i, R_i) because any operation affecting i uses two boundary values, each ≤ the respective side maximum, so the resulting value is bounded by the minimum of those maxima. Hence the formula is optimal.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, overall O( Σ N ) ≤ O(5·10^5 ).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the prefix and suffix arrays (can be reduced to O(1) with two passes, but O(N) fits the limits).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        pref = [0]*n
        suff = [0]*n
        cur = 0
        for i in range(n):
            cur = max(cur, a[i])
            pref[i] = cur
        cur = 0
        for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
            cur = max(cur, a[i])
            suff[i] = cur
        ans = sum(min(pref[i], suff[i]) for i in range(n))
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
